title: The Village That Knew Too Much
description: Elder Theron's briefing, Scholar Milo introduces autobattle, NPC encounters in Harmonia.

steps.title: Chapter 1
steps.subtitle: The Village That Knew Too Much

lines: Harmonia was the kind of village that existed to be left. A waypoint. A place you were from, not a place you stayed.
The bats were bigger. The slimes were angrier. Traders stopped coming through, then stopped sending messages, then stopped existing in conversation entirely.
Elder Theron stood in the village square and regarded the party with a look that suggested they absolutely would not do, but that complaining about it would take energy he didn't have.

lines.speaker: Elder Theron
lines.text: You'll do.

lines.speaker: Bard
lines.text: Such inspiring leadership.

lines.speaker: Elder Theron
lines.text: The cave to the north... something's changed in there. The monsters aren't just wandering anymore. They're organized. Deliberate. You need to find out why.

steps.text: Scholar Milo appeared at the party's elbow with the enthusiasm of a man who had been waiting his entire life for someone to listen to him.

lines.speaker: Scholar Milo
lines.text: Before you go... have you considered the philosophical implications of combat automation?

lines.speaker: Fighter
lines.text: ...What?

lines.speaker: Scholar Milo
lines.text: Imagine a world where you don't fight your battles. You DESIGN them. You build a system of rules — if this, then that — and the system fights for you.

lines.speaker: Scholar Milo
lines.text: Not laziness. Enlightenment.

steps.text: He pressed a leather-bound journal into the Fighter's hands. The cover read: AUTOBATTLE: A Practical Guide to Strategic Detachment.

lines.speaker: Fighter
lines.text: This is a real book?

lines.speaker: Scholar Milo
lines.text: Chapter one is about letting go.

lines.speaker: Scholar Milo
lines.text: Chapter two is about letting go faster.

lines.speaker: Bard
lines.text: What's chapter three?

lines.speaker: Scholar Milo
lines.text: Chapter three is about accepting that the gap between letting go and having it taken from you is smaller than most people think.
He said it with the cheerful tone of a man discussing weather. Nobody thought anything of it. That was what scholars were for.

steps.text: The village offered what it could. A few familiar faces had things to say.

lines.speaker: Phil the Lost
lines.text: Phil. Phil the Lost. Called that because I lose things. Mostly I lose track of... well. Of what's happened before.

lines.speaker: Mage
lines.text: Before what?

lines.speaker: Phil the Lost
lines.text: Before anything much. The village. The seasons. Years blur together. You forget which winter was which.

lines.speaker: Phil the Lost
lines.text: Though I will say this: good luck with the cave. Try not to die in the first room. It's bad for the spirit.

lines.speaker: Fighter
lines.text: The spirit of whom?

lines.speaker: Phil the Lost
lines.text: Unclear.

lines.speaker: Bram
lines.text: Testing dulls the edge! That's the philosophy. Every sword here is in pristine, never-been-used condition.

lines.speaker: Rogue
lines.text: That's... technically backward.

lines.speaker: Bram
lines.text: You look like someone who's going to get hit a lot. Take the shield. Consider it an investment in your surviving long enough to come back and buy more.

lines: The Cleric said a blessing over the equipment. The Mage documented it in his notes as 'ritual application of perceived divine endorsement, efficacy unclear but morale effect measurable.'
The Bard began composing the verse about their departure before they had actually departed.
The Rogue stole a health potion from the back shelf, felt guilty about it, and left a coin that couldn't be afforded.
Elder Theron watched them go with the expression of a man who has planted seeds in difficult soil and has made peace with the uncertainty.
